inline_comments.message: all.yml should not be modified for this. It contains default values. own application.yml should be used that is given on command line for ansible run to populate values.
ansible_host might not be ip address
HOSTS_FILE_RECORD?
NPM_REGISTRY_RECORD or something similar might be better
good point, maybe we should have here cluster_ip instead. Support for that was already merged https://gerrit.onap.org/r/#/c/75452/10/ansible/inventory/hosts.yml

well maybe we can keep it here really empty, but we need to have some real values (even default) for templating script. Without those values templating in onap-casablanca-patch-role will fail.
Furthermore I would like to introduce some example file which can be used by someone, I am quite sure that this practice of not having default values anywhere will leads that people will not fill all values properly and in expected format.
ok
ok

Are we going to patch values.yaml in every deployment ?
Later-on it might cause that we need to recognize every param this file contains to be able to set it properly. Other option is to have some boolean value "patching_onap_values.yaml" or later "patching_robot_values.yaml" and default False ...
Setting-up all OOM parameters is most likely not in scope of offline installer so maybe we should remove this templating or have it possible just for some cases ?

Yes this file really do not belong to this offline installer git.
It's app specific (Onap casablanca Helm charts). I.e. given Helm charts are modified before installed with installer. User could modify own provided Helm charts with all kind of own tools prior to use offline installer.
These fixes should be basically pushed into ONAP Helm charts in some form so that patching is not needed to be used with offline installer. 
Meanwhile it would be good to store this file to somewhere to be usable for anyone wants to use.

This file document what features offline installer supports. In ansible there are multiple ways to provide the values without storing them to git.
If some feature is tried to use where proper values is not filled offline installer will fail and user can notice what is missing.

Then those completely app related parameters (onap_values added here) can be populated in app helm charts.

If I read this right, you agree with me to remove this templating of values.yaml and user is supposed to configure it on his own based on his needs, regarding patching of OOM charts I also agree that storing Patchfile is a kind of workaround until we will be aligned with OOM in Dublin
Why below lines are removed? How is it related to this patch?
I would opt for using git am here instead of just patch to add also some commit messages to sources instead of just changes

do not save this application specific role code here in git.
ansible/application folder is kept empty on git.
This role code is copied at install/runtime to directory defined in ansible.cfg roles_path.


There seems to be now a confusion in installer code i.e. app helm charts are mixed with app specific roles.
Comment shere wrong:
https://gerrit.onap.org/r/gitweb?p=oom/offline-installer.git;a=blob;f=ansible/group_vars/all.yml;h=0a73b63785d2943c9019ed1c4d901751576d2edf;hb=refs/heads/master#l91

and is this the place to move app helm charts to final location https://gerrit.onap.org/r/gitweb?p=oom/offline-installer.git;a=blob;f=ansible/roles/application-install/tasks/pre-install.yml;h=88a95ea68ae06b9bd417caebdec3075bd64b01dd;hb=refs/heads/master#l3 ?

So where app specific roles are transferred to location where ansible can see them?

do not edit this file for the values. Provide separate application_configuration.yml example/template file for the onap Casablanca installation purposes.
Same way as for patches. Create some dir there on top level e.g. onap-conf etc. Save there application_configuration.yml 
 file with proper values filled e.g. this pre_install_role. Also save there the pre install role code in own dir.

Yes those lines should not be removed. 
It's a documentation for user what kind of things he/she can configure. I.e. these lines must be commented out (as they were) if no reasonable working default value can be provided for the parameter in question. And now those are onap specific and related to Openstack so cannot define any defaults but user must define.
This part could be improved a bit with comment to describe 
this more. Now the doc is:

# any other application specific params can be specified in this file
# e.g.
...

Well maybe the confusion is "can be specified in this file". That is true but in practice user should not edit this file but define those params in application_configuration.yml that is given as parameter for the installer run.
And specificly this file's values should not be edited in git (except reasonable default values).
I don't see it's used anywhere or referenced anywhere?

IMHO this is exactly the place where application specific roles like this one should be. Keeping this dir empty is very  confusing, because nobody practically knows how to fill it right. We definitely needs application specific roles like this in git, where would you like to see it instead ? Sorry links you shared are broken. This practice s aligned with  package.sh script (see SW addons)

keeping this file that generic and empty, means that in practice is useless and one has to use some "example but real config instead", this will just introduce additional unnecessary step but I will think about it.
during this review we agreed that we won't be patching onap/values.yaml. this example was covering this case. Keeping example values for something we think that it's not needed doesnt make much sense
it's in newer version of buildguide, see row 301 at https://gerrit.onap.org/r/#/c/77575/5/doc/BuildGuide.rst

patching of helm charts is considered to be a temporary solution, we would like to avoid it in Dublin and try to implement those options directly in upstream OOM. Running own OOM project instead of patching it is quite an overhead

roles will be there in right place during runtime (user do not need to care where), only in git it will be empty. We do not even need the dir in git but it's there for the documentation reasons (README), but when documentation will be created properly on top level we can remove these single README files.
The point is that that place where app helm charts and app roles are copied is internal for the installer not revealed for the user. 
We document for the user properly how to provide helm charts and roles for the installer (package) and it will not be confusing.

I git we can save the app configuration and app roles needed for Casablanca to separate folder on top level (like the patches) and those files can be used if user wants when making package. Those files will be "outside" installer and just one option how to package. Basically these files could be also provided in different git repo.

well this is documentation of available options AND default values for all parameters we reasonable can define some default. I don't think onap-casablanca-patch-role is good generic default value for this. Of course it does not harm as it can be overriden by own config but will be confusing. 

As mentioned in other comment we can save values i.e. application_configuration.yml file known to working with ONAP Casablanca to top level dir together with those app roles files.

as mentioned above this is documentation. Example values can be anything app related onap or something else.
does not matter much are those here or not but they need to be commented out lines anyway. And comment on line 134 we should keep at least.
yes found it later.

our primary goal and afaik 100% of consumers of this oom/offline-installer repo expects that they can install Casablanca ONAP using OOM charts. I am afraid that such push for genericity of installer makes this primary usecase more cumbersome to apply. If we put all changes we need (config from group_vars, casablanca patching role, patchfile) for this installation outside of installer and just try make some instructions in README it will create unnecessary extra (manual) step and potential for failure. I am not against writing the installer generic so it can be reused later for other cases, but just when it's not complicating it's primary purpose. What about OOM PTL to decide here ? Both these views are based on our understanding of expectations from the community, comments of people outside of our group might be very beneficial.

On the contrary the aim is try to make it more clear and easier to use. I.e. ONAP Casablanca needed configuration in one place and not in many places deep inside the git repo structure.
This is very basic separation of code (installer) and the configuration. All the needed generality is already there and no change on that or making it more generic. Just using it in the way it was designed. 
The other cases you refer is probably something else than ONAP? This is not the intended use case I'm after here (even if it will help on that also), just separate onap Casablanca configuration. Other use cases could be ONAP Beijing, ONAP Dublin and ONAP with different components and different configs (different patch roles).

each release is having different expectations from the installer (mostly inside ./roles/application ) and we can easily cover it by following same branching strategy with other ONAP projects. All necessary adaptations will come "for free" in respective branch. Keeping installer "generic" means same code can be run for different ONAP versions creates unnecessary overhead.
What will be the benefit of it ?? Anyone can simply use code tailored for specific release in respective branch. So there is no benefit. On the other hand code will be less readable and clear instructions will be replaced by "generic sayings" like use the right number of k8s nodes for your specific release. 
More examples: a) nfs handling for dockerdata config - mandatory is R2, optional in R3, irrelevant in R4
b) helm install / helm plugins c) download scripts - we can keep even same names of all files, in generic case everything will comes from some parameter, d) library/rancher_k8s_environment module - this is tailored to  specific rancher version, it is an overhead of making it compatible with all versions of rancher used, isn't it ? 
Practically any single change we need to apply for current release is less cost & effort than the same change if we need to keep the same code compatible with older release.
Any example above can be covered by introducing some more complexity but what's the point of making it that complicated ?

None of the things you described is related to this commit and my comments for it. I already said before that my comments are not related to this kind of "generic" you think here and for some reason hate/fear without grounds. 
So let's keep this in the actual matter.

Comments was to keep app configuration in separate place instead of inserting it into installer code (defaults) itself. That is just the way this installer is designed to be used nothing more. The config will be in git and that same config same files can be overwritten in each onap branch according to onap projects like you described. And all those "free" stuff will apply. We don't need to keep config files of all onap releases in all branches, it's not forcing it, it's completely independent choice.

Maybe some other top level dir would be better for this file.
It's needed to conf whole onap deployment not only patching.

onap-conf
onap-values
onap-parameters

Do we want to keep it and keep changing the dir/role name in each onap release? Of course this is minor as we may get rid of the pactching. But still different if otherwice we will keep files in branch targeted to that branch (release).

both ansible role and patchfile is there for patching. The only exception is example configuration file which is kind of example file with patching role .... honestly I still likes "patches" a bit more than onap-<> because it's better fitting to content
we can remove once your is merged, not earlier

honestly honestly.
This "example" configuration file is the whole point. That defines all values how onap Casablanca should be installed and what configuration we are testing.
Patching staff is part of that configuration for sure, and at the moment you see only that, but all other lines on this config file are equally important.
